text: The surgeon identifies and removes a level nine lymph node, which is visually distinct within the surgical field.
The lung is retracted medially, revealing the posterior hilum, while the pleura overlying the subcranial space is carefully mobilized.
A piece of gauze is placed in the subcranial space, followed by the surgeon proceeding to the right upper lobe bronchus.
The surgeon mobilizes the pleura from the right upper lobe bronchus and dissects the azygos vein away from the right upper lobe.
The surgeon retracts the lung downward towards the diaphragm, exposing the apical aspect of the lung.
The surgeon dissects the azygos vein away from surrounding structures, and removes a level ten lymph node located along the pulmonary artery near the truncus anterior for pathological examination.
The surgeon identifies the segmental anatomy, with the apical vein of the right upper lobe coming into view. The vein is carefully dissected and then transected using a vascular stapler.
The surgeon dissects around the truncus to identify the apical branch, specifically the A1 branch, leading to the lobe segment.
The apical branch is visible in the surgical field, highlighted against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a vascular load to transect the identified branch, completing the dissection of the segmental nodes and small vessels.
The surgeon focuses on the apical bronchus segment, preparing to dissect or manipulate this anatomical structure.
The surgeon folds the lung downward to expose the apex, revealing the apical segment as they maneuver around it.
The surgeon performs a test inflation of the lung, visually confirming aeration in both the posterior and anterior segments.
The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to visually demarcate the segmental fissure, followed by marking the demarcation with bipolar cautery.
The surgeon uses multiple green loads of the stapler to wedge out the apical segment of the right upper lobe, completing the transection of this lung segment.
The surgeon flips the lung back and forth between each firing of the stapler to ensure the bronchus, vein, and artery are included within the staple line.
The surgeon uses black loads of the stapler to transect the thick tissue within the segmental fissure, facilitating the separation of the fissure with improved precision.
The surgeon approaches the final fissure to complete the segmentectomy, using surgical instruments to ensure precise separation and removal of the targeted lung segment.
The surgeon presents the excised specimen, likely the appendix, following the completion of the appendectomy.
